title: Freher's Process in the Philosophical Work: Spiritual Anatomy and the Path of Redemption
subtitle: Dionysius Andreas Freher's Commentary on Jacob Boehme's Signatura Rerum — the Seven Properties of Nature, the Heavenly and Earthly Mercury, and the Parallel of Alchemy and Regeneration

summary.one_line: Freher's 40-position treatise on the alchemical process as a direct analogue of Christian regeneration — the Heavenly Mercury, the conjunction of fire and water, and the seven properties of nature ...
summary.scope: Full text of Freher's 'Process in the Philosophical Work considered as thoroughly analogical with that in Man's Redemption' from MS. Ashmole, covering the paradisical state and Fall of Adam, the role of the Heavenly Mercury, the Philosophical Baptism, the Temptation in the Wilderness, the Three Murderers of Venus, and the seven Properties of Nature as exemplified in Christ's miracles.

sources.label: Freher's Process in the Philosophical Work — from MS. Ashmole, published on the Alchemy Website

body.text: As long as Adam stood in a pure paradisical innocency, the Eternal Word and power of life (called by the author the Heavenly Mercury), was his leader, and had pre-dominance in him. His life, which was a clear flaming fire, burned in and was nourished by that pure spiritual oil of the Divine substantiality; which, together with the holy water of eternal life, is generated in the angelical world: and this, therefore, could not but give forth a glorious bright shining light. Through the power of his imagination, or lust after the knowledge of good and evil, that which then was still kept under in him, and was so hidden from him, viz., the outward watery property, came to be manifest in his holy oil, and got predominance therein. This oil therefore, now overpowered thereby, could no more be such an agreeable food, and well-doing to his fire, as it could and did before. And so his fire not only lost its shining light, but came also to be spoiled itself, for it was obscured, and made all impotent. And his Mercury, which before in his holy oil, had caused and raised up paradisical joy and triumph, according to his moving and stirring property, was now made a stinging anguishing poison, according to his own natural constitution, which he doth and must stand in, when before or without the light.

Immediately after the fall of Man, God said unto the serpent, I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed: her seed shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel. And herein the philosopher's stone or tincture lieth implicitly. For though this primarily concerneth Man, yet secondarily it concerneth the whole Creation also; and this bruising of the serpent's head is done both spiritually and corporeally, and both in time and in eternity, and though in different degrees, yet in a parallel process or method, both here and there. The serpent's sting points at the Wrath-fire, and the woman's seed at the Light and Love-fire. These two are in every thing: and in the curse that former came to be predominant in outward Nature. This latter must now be raised up again, and, by its shining through the Wrath, it must subdue and keep it under, and take away from it its predominant power, so that it may keep and exercise only its true natural office, as a servant in and to the light.

The poor fallen humanity, considered so barely as it was in and to itself, viz., as broken, spoiled, poisoned, was not cast immediately into the fiery furnace, and melted down by the Wrath of God; but a conjunction was first made between the Earthly and Heavenly humanity. First, the humanity was to be baptized with water in the Jordan, and with water from above the firmament. Further it was led into the wilderness, for to be tempted by the Devil. This Philosophical Baptism is nothing else but a conjunction, to be made between the fiery and watery Mercury. The fiery must be baptized with the watery. And this is what Behmen means by saying obscurely: 'Have a care only for this, that thou baptisest the mercury with his own baptism.' For this watery Mercury is his own, viz., it is that, which before the Fall and Curse he enjoyed and rejoiced in, as his most precious treasure; whereby his fiery poisonous Wrath, was kept under, and prevented from being manifest.

Freher's 'Heavenly Mercury' — the eternal Word and power of life that was Adam's leader before the Fall — is the same daimonic pneuma that Synesius describes as the 'medium of daimonic impression' (De Insomniis). In the language of the Tantraloka (5.12-15), this is the shuddha-vidya-tattva, the pure cognitive principle that shines through the veils of malina when the practitioner has stabilized the prana. The 'poisoned Mercury' that results from the Fall is the state of ahamkara (egoic contraction) that the Layayoga seeks to dissolve through mantra and breath. The 'outward watery property' that gained predominance corresponds to the apas-tattva in its bhautika (gross) aspect, which Steiner describes as the etheric body's densification after the 'Fall into matter.' The process of restoring the Heavenly Mercury is identical to the reversal of the tattvic outflow that the Sat-cakra-nirupana prescribes through the awakening of kundalini.

Freher's 'Philosophical Baptism' — the conjunction of fiery and watery Mercury — is the alchemical formulation of what the Tantraloka calls diksha (initiation), specifically the 'baptism of the seed-mantra' (bija-nyasa). The fiery Mercury is the prana-shakti in its solar (pingala) aspect; the watery Mercury is the lunar (ida) current. Their conjunction in the susumna is the 'baptism in the Jordan' — the flow of the central channel that purifies the nadis. The 'forty days in the wilderness' corresponds to the forty-day mandala practice described in the PGM (the 'forty-day theurgic retreat'), during which the practitioner's pneuma is purified and the daimon manifests. In Steiner's anthroposophy, this is the period of 'soul-calisthenics' needed to purify the astral body before higher perception is possible. The warning that the Artist must first experience the regeneration in himself before he can perform it in metals is the same principle as the guru's requirement in the Kaula tradition: one must have attained the state before one can transmit it.

Freher's identification of Saturn, Mercury, and Mars as the 'three murderers of Venus' who correspond to the Civil, Ecclesiastical, and Diabolical powers that crucified Christ is a Boehmist formulation of the three gunas. Saturn (tamas — inertia, the 'dark astringent property') is the civil government that sentenced Christ; Mercury (rajas — the mobile, restless, 'ecclesiastical' quality) is the high priest's party that prosecuted him; Mars (the fiery, destructive aspect of rajas that turns to tamas — the Devil) is the active principle of opposition. Venus herself is sattva — the luminous, loving quality that stands 'from above' against the three 'from beneath.' The transmutation of these three into harmony with Venus is the gunatita state described in the Bhagavata Purana and the Layayoga: the state beyond the three gunas, where the purified mind no longer oscillates between inertia, activity, and luminosity but rests in the pure consciousness (shuddha-caitanya). Steiner describes this as the 'three soul forces' (thinking, feeling, willing) being harmonized through initiation.

gaps: The positions dealing with the seven Properties of Nature and Christ's miracles need fuller extraction
The detailed instructions on the fire regimen and the 40-day temptation process need further extraction

issues.title: Passages are extracts not full verbatim text
issues.description: Current passages summarize key positions. Full text of Freher's 40 positions should be added with references.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Freher's Process in the Philosophical Work. Six passages cover: the paradisical state and Fall, the serpent and tincture, and the Philosophical Baptism, with commentary connecting to the daimonic pneuma, Tantric diksha, and the three gunas.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Freher's Process text
